feed.title: SAP Community - SAP Community
feed.subtitle: SAP Community blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: Personalized SAP Community RSS Feeds: Open Source Tool for Following Managed Tags, Contributor
entry.content:
Bringing Back RSS Feeds to the SAP Community

Do you miss the good old days when you could easily follow managed tags like "ABAP Development" and receive all the blog posts associated with that tag? Or when you could follow individual contributors via RSS? Well, I certainly did! That's why I decided to build my own RSS server to bring back this functionality and offer a SAP Community RSS Feed API!

The key advantage of this server is that it doesn’t rely on pre-generated feeds. Instead, you can customize and generate your own feed with precision.

Getting Started

For example, to follow the "ABAP Development" tag, you can use this URL:

https://rss-scn.marianzeis.de/api/messages?conversation.style=blog&managedTag.title=ABAP%20Development&feeds.replies=false

Want to include comments in your feed? Just set feeds.replies to true.

Interested only in Q&A? Use:

https://rss-scn.marianzeis.de/api/messages?conversation.style=qanda&managedTag.title=ABAP%20Development&feeds.replies=false

You can even follow specific users, like the amazing @qmacro . Simply use their user ID, for example:

https://rss-scn.marianzeis.de/api/messages?author.id=53

To simplify working with the parameters, I’ve provided a Swagger interface:

Swagger UI for SAP Community RSS Feed API

 

 

 

Features

This project is, of course, open source! Check it out on GitHub:

GitHub Repository: rss-scn

Some of the key features include:

  • Customize your RSS feeds by various parameters.
  • Follow specific tags, authors, or content types.
  • Include or exclude replies in your feeds.
  • Swagger documentation available at /api-docs.
  • Automatic deployment ensures the latest updates are always live.

How It Works

The SAP Community platform provides an API that allows access to its content. My RSS server acts as a bridge between this API and your RSS reader. When you make a request to my server with specific parameters, it fetches the relevant content from the SAP Community API, formats it into an RSS feed, and serves it back to you.

You don't need to be familiar with programming to use it. Just construct the URL with the parameters you want, and your RSS reader will handle the rest. The parameters allow you to filter content by author, tag, content type (like blogs or Q&A), and whether to include replies.

Examples of Usage

Let's say you're interested in all blog posts related to "ABAP Development" but don't want to see the replies. You'd use this URL:

https://rss-scn.marianzeis.de/api/messages?conversation.style=blog&managedTag.title=ABAP%20Development&feeds.replies=false

If you also want to include replies or comments, simply change feeds.replies to true:

https://rss-scn.marianzeis.de/api/messages?conversation.style=blog&managedTag.title=ABAP%20Development&feeds.replies=true

Interested in questions and answers instead of blogs? Adjust the conversation.style parameter:

https://rss-scn.marianzeis.de/api/messages?conversation.style=qanda&managedTag.title=ABAP%20Development&feeds.replies=false

To follow a specific author, use their author ID:

https://rss-scn.marianzeis.de/api/messages?author.id=53

Using the Swagger Interface

To make it even easier, there's a Swagger interface where you can interactively build your query without worrying about the exact URL syntax. You can access it here:

Swagger UI for SAP Community RSS Feed API

In the Swagger UI, you'll find a form where you can input your desired parameters. It will show you the exact URL to use and even let you test the API directly from the browser.

Creating an OPML File from Your SAP Community Following

If you want to import all the people you follow on SAP Community into your RSS reader, I've created a script that generates an OPML file—a format supported by most RSS readers for bulk import of feeds.

You can find the script and instructions here:

Create OPML from SAP Community Following

If you prefer, you can also use my sample OPML file, which includes a selection of popular contributors:

followers_feeds_sample.opml

Why I Built This

I was inspired by a post from Antonio Maradiaga titled "Exploring the RSS feeds of the new SAP Community platform". In his post, Antonio discusses how the new SAP Community platform has changed the way RSS feeds work, and some of the challenges in accessing them.

He pointed out that while the new platform offers some RSS capabilities, they are not as straightforward or flexible as they used to be. For instance, following individual contributors via RSS isn't readily available. You can read his full post here:

Exploring the RSS feeds of the new SAP Community platform

Wanting to bring back the convenience and customization of the old RSS feeds, I decided to create a solution that fills this gap. My RSS Feed API provides a more flexible way to access the content you care about.

Behind the Scenes

While you don't need to understand the technical details to use the service, here's a high-level overview of how it works:

  • API Integration: The server interacts with the SAP Community API to fetch the latest content based on your specified parameters.
  • Data Formatting: It takes the raw data from the API and formats it into an RSS feed, which is a standardized format that RSS readers can interpret.
  • Custom Parameters: By adjusting the query parameters in the URL, you can tailor the feed to include exactly what you're interested in—be it specific tags, authors, content types, or whether to include replies.
  • Open Source: The entire project is open source, meaning the code is publicly available for anyone to inspect, contribute to, or modify. This transparency ensures that the community can trust and improve the tool.
  • Automatic Updates: The server is set up to automatically update and deploy any changes, ensuring that you always have access to the latest features and fixes.

Conclusion

I hope you find this RSS Feed API as useful as I do! It's designed to bring back the flexibility and convenience of RSS feeds in the SAP Community, allowing you to stay updated with the content that matters most to you.

If you have any feedback, suggestions, or need assistance, feel free to reach out or contribute to the project on GitHub.

entry.title: SAP Academic Community Conference Ibero-America 2024: What an Experience!
entry.content:
The SAP Academic Community Conference Ibero-America 2024  was an event to remember!

As you know, a key mission of SAP University Alliances is to build bridges between SAP, educational institutions, and the SAP ecosystem, and your engagement brings this vision to life! We were thrilled to have you with us for both our virtual sessions and at each of the six on-premises gatherings across SAP offices in Latin America.

Already feeling nostalgic for those #SAPACC moments? We’ve put together a quick recap to relive some of the highlights and celebrate the insights, discussions, and connections that made this event memorable...

 

#SAPACC recap – November 4th

Virtual Webinar

Our #SAPACC week kicked off with an inspiring webinar introducing SAP Next-Gen, showcasing our offerings to the academic community, and featuring several special sessions with SAP leaders on various key segments.

With over 190 participants, 8 hours of content, and 7 sessions, the event provided a unique opportunity to explore SAP’s of and the diverse opportunities for academic collaboration.

Participants explored key topics such as Cloud, Artificial Intelligence (AI), Sustainability, and Diversity & Inclusion (D&I), while also connecting with other members of our community. This session highlighted the valuable resources within SAP University Alliances that support your journey both in and beyond the classroom!

Looking for a recap of any sessions? You can access recordings and slides here: 

  • Opening: Welcome to the SAP Next-Gen World with Dr. Karina Edmonds  
  • SAP University Alliances with Maria Calzada & Gloria Sandi 
  • Academic Board Ibero-America with Jesus Aguillar 
  • Cloud at SAP with Alexandre Pereira & special guest
  • Sustainability at SAP with Renata Amorim 
  • AI at SAP with Matheus Souza 
  • Diversity and Inclusion at SAP with Ezequiel Massa 
  • Slides

 

#SAPACC recap – November 8th

Bogotá, Colombia

Our Bogotá event brought together 25 participants, who engaged in seven insightful sessions led by SAP experts and special panelists from Universidad de Los Andes and ERP4students.

Buenos Aires, Argentina

In Buenos Aires, 13 attendees participated in eight sessions. Contributions came from SAP experts, alongside panel discussions featuring leading institutions such as UNTREF, UADE, ERPsimLab, and a special appearance by Tecnológico de Monterrey.

Lima, Peru

Our event in Lima welcomed 24 participants who took part in four engaging sessions led by SAP experts and complemented by panels with insights from special guests from Universidad Peruana de Ciencias Aplicadas and Universidad de Lima.

Monterrey, Mexico

The Monterrey event brought together 24 attendees with an agenda featuring seven sessions presented by SAP experts and special panelists from Tecnológico de Monterrey offering valuable insights.

Santiago, Chile

In Santiago, 20 participants joined us for five sessions, featuring perspectives from our guest panelists from Universidad de las Américas and Universidad Bernardo O'Higgins, along with other special sessions led by SAP experts.

São Leopoldo, Brazil

Our largest event, in São Leopoldo, gathered 66 participants for ten inspiring sessions with SAP experts and contributions from professors from leading institutions such as FESP, FSA, Mackenzie, UCS, and UFRGS.

 

Thank you once again to everyone who attended our events, especially those who submitted panels and contributed their incredible insights and knowledge to the SAP Academic Community. It was a true pleasure to engage with each of you and learn about the amazing work being done, as well as the exciting plans for the future!

Share your favorite moments or key takeaways from the event in the comments below. We look forward to continuing to work together and strengthen our collaboration moving forward 💙

entry.title: SAP Inside Track Barcelona 2024 Recap
entry.content:
We would like to start our recap with a sincere gratituted to all who contributed making it happen, our attendees, speakers, sponsors and volunteers. This year 2024 with 80+ attendees that made SITBarcelona gathering incredible.

On Thursday evening, staff, friends and speakers of SITBarcelona enjoyed an alltogether informal dinner in a relaxed and friendly atmosphere before the event.

     

This eighth edition of SAP Inside Track Barcelona featured a diverse and engaging program. It included presentations by SAP experts and members of the SAP Community Network, touching on a variety of cutting-edge topics.

Antonio AncosSAP 2024 de la A a la Z
Antonio LeiteKnowledge Graphs with SAP Data
Josetxu FernandezGenAI in SAP Data and Analytics
Fran Villegas

Get Business insights from your SAP data with Natural Language and Develop Gen AI solutions integrating AWS and SAP BTP Services

Alejandro RodriguezSAP BTP Chef: first SAP demo where all the pillars of BTP are shown in a practical business case.
Felipe BorgesGlobal eCompliance with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance
Gregory DoukasTechnician Training Tool powered by Apple Vision Pro and SAP S/4HANA
Maria Codinach and Harsh KumarFrit Ravich's success case in the modernization of sales application: from SMP to in-house middleware
Niranjan GattupalliImplementing gCTS for Continous Delivery in complex S/4HANA landscapes: Branching Models, Hotfix strategies and 5-Tier Architecture
Antonio ValladaresIntegration Suite addon build in SAP CAP by Code10
Bernhard LueckeReinventing Support with AI

Between the sessions at the breaks, were made several raffle to win 2 e-books and 1month free subscription to all attendees thanks to Espresso Tutorials, 2 e-books thanks to SAP PRESS and 5 100€ Gift Cards from SAP Store thanks to CUVIV. Here we would like to thank so much our beloved SAP Champions and friend Nil Peksen who accepted to be on stage and run some of the draws.

In addition to the insightful presentations, the event also featured a hands-on session led by Antonio Maradiaga, a member of the SAP Developer Advocates. This practical session focused on SAP Build Code and demonstrated how to develop applications using Generative AI and SAP's Joule Assistant.

As every past event, remember that all content was recorded and now availabe here. And also all official photos will be published in this album for you.

We would like to specially thank you to SAP people from Spain like  Luis Felipe Lanz, Bernhard Luecke, Jorge Perez, Cesar Martin, Antonio Maradiaga, Rafael Valdes and Carles Viaplana for their continous engament with our local SAP Community and supporting the event to take it to the next level.

As said before, big thanks to all our sponsors who year after year believe and support our SAP Community event:

The event concluded with a great sense of excitement, leaving everyone eagerly anticipating next year’s edition. A heartfelt thanks to all attendees, speakers, sponsors, and volunteers who made it all possible.

SAP Inside Track Barcelona continues to prove itself as one of the most influential events in the SAP community, offering a platform for knowledge sharing, networking, and exploring the latest innovations in the SAP ecosystem.

So, looking forward to meet all of you in SAP Inside Track Barcelona 2025!!

Mireia Bubi, Lluis Salvador, Enric Castella, Losel Tenzin, Imma Salmeron, David Ruiz

entry.title: How to add a Co-Author to your blog post?
entry.content:
It is possible to add another SAP community member as a Co-Author to your blog post. 

This functionality comes handy e.g. for SAP employees that start to work on other technical topics within SAP but might also be of interest for others as well, e.g. when you want that somebody maintains your blog post because you are on vacation.  

To do so you simply start to edit your blog post by selecting "Edit Article" from the drop down list.   

  

Then you have to scroll down and you will find on the right hand side on the bottom of the page the "Co-Authors" box.  

The tricky thing is now to find the correct username your colleague might have in the SAP community but you can get this from simply copying the same from other blog posts he or she has posted.  

Here I have taken my colleague @CarineTchoutouo as an example.  

As a result Carine now shows up as a Co-Author and would be able to change my post if I am on vacation.

entry.title: SAPience user day - a breath of fresh air
entry.content:
How to simplify your work every day

SAPience represents the Belgian SAP user group. Last Tuesday 03/11/2024 they hosted the SAPience user day in  Salons De Waerboom in Dilbeek. Since one year new leadership is in place and change has been ongoing to transform the user group.

You can find more information on SAPience over on their website: https://sapience.be/

The theme of this year was "How to simplify your work every day".

There are also new, additional board members in place and all of this seems to have led to what I would call a breath of fresh air: new ideas, new initiatives and as a result new outcomes.

I was present together with SAP Partner CNT with whom Aiden partners up for a number of projects since we are often complementary. A collaboration that works very well and creates win-win situations for both parties.

The day kicked off with announcements around SAPience, followed by a short introduction into where SAP stands in terms of strategy by Stijn De Beuckelaer.

Then it was center stage for a lengthier presentation on SAP Business AI by Kai Muehlbauer. You could sense that the AI topic is hot, some of the audience was shifting more towards the tip of their chair, others were wipping out cellphones to take pictures of the slides.

A small demo near the end was in my opinion the most impressive, SAP's Joule AI premium skills used in combination with each other across SAP's product portfolio speaks to the imagination of users on what the future can look like with the help of AI assistance. It screams "this is how it should be" if you would ask me to describe what it looks like. Pretty powerful in other words. The future looks bright.

Break-out sessions by SAP Partners and customers

After the keynote you had multiple tracks of break-out sessions by SAP Partners and customers on how they have simplified their every day work using SAP technology.

Outside of networking, I've attended a number of sessions and what I found was a lot of willingness to share knowledge, a lot of enthusiasm and a large community spirit. Basically what I would expect from a SAP user group where you would find a lot of individuals who really love being active in this space. It was in contrast with my experience of a number of years ago.

Networking opportunities

The enthusiasm from within the sessions also translated into a lot of conversations and networking outside of the sessions. The event was well organized, the venue was accommodating and there was ample room for networking in terms of time divided between sessions and breaks.

Opportunity to grow and call for action

I had the pleasure of meeting a lot of friends there from within the Belgian SAP community. You could state the usual suspects were present but still I was missing some key persons so that's a call for action really.

From what I've seen at the event, there is much more that can happen still in the future given the user group keeps on growing. I've already expressed my interest to get involved in terms of giving knowledge sessions and presentations in the future and being active in the Belgian user group.

You can see the new branding already in the header of this blog and it represents (for me) the breath of fresh air that is now installed in SAPience so a call out to SAP Partners and customers in Belgium: reach out to the SAPience user group if you're not yet a member and consider becoming a member because you can find added value here and strength does also come in numbers in terms of sharing knowledge to each other and discussing amongst peers on best practices and more when it comes to SAP.

entry.title: Author Spotlight: Daniel Mateo - December 2024 Edition
entry.content:
You might have seen our first launch of recognizing active authors in SAP Community with Tim Koster. I am thrilled to share our second interview with @DanielMateo with you, who is already part of SAP Community for more than 15 years. Read more about his background, his experiences at recent events, and his main interests. Remember, this is all about Dani!   

 

Svea: Hi Dani, really nice to meet you and thanks for agreeing to be part of this initiative. Let’s talk about you: how and when did you get in SAP Community?

Dani: Hello and thanks for inviting me. It is an absolute pleasure to participate in this initiative. More than 15 years ago I got certified in SAP Supply Chain Management and I got user access to the SAP Community Network (SCN). At that time, I used to review doubts about configuration in SAP Finance and Logistics or issues before raising an OSS note.

I think it was around 8 years ago when SCN moved to a more user-friendly website, community.sap.com. It was then easier to build interactions and follow some friends or topics.

Some months ago, SAP Community was migrated into a new platform. The blogging tool got changed, Groups appeared, and it looks now easier and more structured to find posts by areas of knowledge.

Svea: That is a long history. What does the community mean to you?

Dani: SAP Community is the best way to be informed about the most trending topics related to SAP solutions and multiple cloud tools, considering the main features and improvements in each periodic release. The posts are of good quality with official sources, and they are reviewed, so it ensures that it is high-quality content. I also like that it contains both: posts from SAP employees, but also from other experts. Both are providing valuable insights about SAP functionalities.

In my case, it is also having visibility in the SAP network while sharing posts about specific areas of experience, for example finance. And to be kept updated about upcoming online and onsite events, or summary of past events. 

Svea: What inspires you most? As you mentioned events, have you recently joined any?

Dani: I enjoy creating blog posts in the SAP Community to inform members about finance and controlling functionalities. I regularly review updates from SAP colleagues who generate high quality content, and stay in touch with them.

I also usually participate in SAP Inside Track events that are oftentimes organized by external community members. I was a speaker at SIT Fortaleza and Barcelona, and I also joined them already as a participant. I am even close friends with the organizer of SAP Inside Track Barcelona. I joined several SAP Stammtisch there, which have been started during the pandemic. They have been paused for a while, but we want to start them again, to stay connected throughout the year. 

Svea: That is really cool. Would you want to tell a bit more about your background?

Dani: Sure. It is quite interesting because I studied Chemical Engineering and Master of Business Administration. I always liked computers and around 18 years ago after I finished my degree, I started in an IT Consulting firm as an ABAP developer. I was interested in improving my knowledge about logistics and finance processes, and I agreed to take further responsibilities, and finally took over a role as an SAP functional Consultant in Finance and Logistics. Since that time, I have participated in several SAP projects.

Svea: Nice that you changed your career.

Is there any other “highlight” you want to share?

Dani: As I mentioned, I have over 18 years of experience with SAP topics. During that time, I focused on international SAP projects. For example, I participated in multiple rollouts of finance, controlling, and logistics topics in America, Asia, and Europe. Recently, I participated in writing a book as a co-author called “Strategies and best practices in SAP S/4HANA rollouts” in Spanish language.


[image: https://www.espresso-tutorials.de/produkt/estrategias-y-mejores-practicas-en-despliegues-de-sap-s-4hana/]

Svea: Wow, that is incredible! Do you have any plans for your future in SAP Community?

Dani: Of course. I want to keep sharing posts about Finance and Controlling features in SAP S/4HANA On-Premise and Public Cloud solutions. I also want to improve my networking with my contacts and participate in further SAP Stammtisch or SAP Inside Track events.

Svea: I am happy to hear that. On the other hand: what would you like to get from SAP Community or other community members?

Dani: That they continue to inform about latest trends and best practices for implementing SAP, about new functionalities in releases in Public Cloud for Finance and Logistics. But also having an updated calendar about online events like SAP Inside Tracks, SAP Stammtisch, or any other community events.

Furthermore, and I am just thinking out loud, it would be great to get some give aways, some SAP merchandise. Maybe as an award, a recognition, to keep all participants and members engaged and also motivated to continue sharing knowledge in SAP Community.

 

Svea: Good idea, I keep this in mind 😉

Is there anything else you would like to share?

Dani: I can only thank the SAP Community for all the engagement. I want to thank SAP colleagues for using the community to share useful information. Thanks to all members, who organize events, and I hope they will continue so we will get further insights about SAP functionalities in the future as well.

Svea: Thank you, Dani, for sharing these insights. It was a pleasure having this conversation with you.

entry.title: Enhancements for SAP Community - December 2024
entry.content:
I am honored to be picking up the mantle of sharing news regarding enhancements (meaning improvements and bug fixes) to the SAP Community. Following are the enhancements released since the last posting.

Privacy Statement

Please note that the Privacy Statement for the SAP Community has been revised.  An outdated paragraph referencing an earlier AI capability has been removed.  In its place, there is a section that addresses the inclusion of search results based upon generative AI processing.  In the document, this is referred to as “SAP Consulting Capability.”

UI and Bug Fixes

  • Mobile UI

    • Ensured there was a sufficient gap between ''Start a discussion'/'Create a blog' and feed sorting drop-down list on feed pages.  This improves visibility and clarity of the drop-down list.

 

  • Latest Post UI
    • The use of different green check marks (see Before image) serves only to confuse the user as they attempt to discern the reason for the different images.  Now, a consistent graphic is used to represent an accepted solution in Latest Post widget in Question pages.   Where do we configure SAP FSM Item types? - SAP Community

  • Mobile experience scrolling issue was fixed.
    • Users had difficulty scrolling down the page on a mobile device to get to posts. This issue has been fixed.

 

Posting Comment or Answer on Question

Users have been posting comments as the answer to a question.  With this fix, it is now easier to differentiate between answering a question and commenting on a question as the distinction between the two has been made clearer with additional verbiage around each option.

 

Custom content block in right column to group landing page

A custom content component is available on the group landing page to provide additional information or resources.

 

Sort Content on Boards in Groups by Creation Date

To make it easier to check the newest questions/blogs, a new Sort By option has been added to the drop-down list.  Users can now sort by Creation Date.

 

Community Events Page

Event creators can now select one or multiple industries as a ‘Label’ when creating an event.  This now enables the user to filter events by Labels and find events that are relevant to their interests and needs.

entry.title: SAP Inside Track Porto 2025: Connecting SAP Enthusiasts Globally
entry.content:
SAP Inside Track Porto 2025: Connecting SAP Enthusiasts Globally

SAP Inside Track (SIT) Porto 2025 is part of a well-established global series of community-driven events designed to foster collaboration and knowledge-sharing among SAP professionals. Taking place on September 27, 2025, at The Lodge Hotel in Vila Nova de Gaia, Portugal, the event is expected to attract a mix of seasoned experts and newcomers eager to deepen their SAP expertise.

What is SAP Inside Track?

SAP Inside Track events, as detailed in the SAP Community Blog, are grassroots initiatives organized by members of the SAP community. These events emphasize collaboration, providing a platform for SAP professionals to:

  • Share Knowledge: Presentations and hands-on workshops from SAP experts and community members.
  • Network: Opportunities to connect with peers, mentors, and industry leaders.
  • Stay Updated: Insights into SAP’s latest innovations, including topics like SAP S/4HANA, AI, sustainability, and instant payments.

Each SAP Inside Track is unique, tailored to the local community's needs and interests. Events are informal and encourage open dialogue, breaking away from traditional conference formats.

Porto 2025 Highlights

  1. Diverse Agenda:

    • Sessions on generative AI, sustainability through SAP Green Ledger, and advanced ERP transformations like SAP S/4HANA.
    • Discussions on industry-specific SAP use cases.

  2. Inclusivity:

    • Opportunities for attendees to volunteer or even host sessions, reinforcing the community-driven nature of the event.

  3. Global Context:

    • SAP Inside Track events are hosted in various cities worldwide, each reflecting its local culture while contributing to the global SAP network.

  4. Event Details:

    • Date: September 27, 2025
    • Location: The Lodge Hotel, Rua de Serpa Pinto, Vila Nova de Gaia, Portugal
    • Access: Registration, with full access to lectures and networking opportunities.

Why Attend?

SAP Inside Track Porto 2025 offers more than technical insights; it’s an opportunity to connect, innovate, and grow within the SAP ecosystem. For anyone passionate about SAP technologies, this event bridges the gap between knowledge and practical application.

For more details on SAP Inside Track events, visit the SAP Community Blog and the Porto event page.

entry.title: SAP Inside Track Tokyo 2025 Event Information
entry.content:
SAP Inside Track Tokyo 2025 event announcement in Japanese.

※登壇者募集中
※参加者募集は、12月中旬以降を予定しています

SAP TechEd 、TechEd Japan 、SAP Inside Track Tokyo の季節となりました！
SAP Inside Track はSAP関連トピックを共有し合う、草の根コミュニティイベントです。

イベント名： SAP Inside Track Tokyo 2025
テーマ　　： Connect Together, Better Together ～ ともに繋がり、ともに良くなる
言語　　　： 日本語
開催形式　： ハイブリット開催
開催期間　： 2025年2月28日(金)、3月14日(金)、3月19日(水)　※全3回
開催時間　： 19:00～22:00予定
参加費　　： 無料

SAP Community に登録すればどなたでも参加できます！
学生、フリーランス、ユーザ企業、パートナー企業、JSUG会員、SAP社員など自由参加です。
※まだコミュニティメンバーでない方はこちら

イベントのねらい

SAP TechEdやSAP TechEd Japanで学んだこと、プロジェクトで経験したことをコミュニティで共有することで、みんなで学びあいませんか？
アウトプットがあなたの活動の幅を広げます！

参加申し込み

 

セッション概要

ハッシュタグ　：#sitTokyo
運営　　　　　：chillSAP　※SAP社のイベントではなくコミュニティのカジュアルイベントです
スポンサー　　：未定
関連イベント　：SAP TechEd 2024、SAP TechEd Japan 2024

 

SAP Inside Track Tokyo 2025 セッション情報

1. セッション内容

  • 持ち時間15分のプレゼン、デモ、考察
  • 元ネタは SAP TechEd 2024 、 SAP TechEd Japan 2024 で学んだこと、プロジェクトで経験したこと
  • TechEd のトピック考察、私はこう思う
  • TechEd に参加して深堀りしてみた（SAP TechEd on Tourに参加された方もオンラインで参加された方も是非！）
  • TechEd のトピック試してみた、ハンズオンやってみた
  • プロジェクトで経験して、こう使うといい、ここが要改善など

 

2. スケジュールとトラックのトピックス

Day

日程

トラック

トピック

動画

Day 12025年2月28日(金)Digital Transformation with Cloud ERP (DT)未定 
  Artificial Intelligence (AI)未定 
Day 22025年3月14日(金)Application Development and Automation (AD)未定 
  Integration (IN)未定 
Day 32025年3月19日(水)Data and Analytics (DA)未定 

 

3. 登壇者＆発表コンテンツ

※発表コンテンツは随時更新中です！

2/28 Day 1「Digital Transformation with Cloud ERP (DT) & Artificial Intelligence (AI)」

NameTitle
PhotoOverview

3/14 Day 2「Application Development and Automation (AD) & Integration (IN)」

NameTitle
PhotoOverview

3/19 Day 3「Data and Analytics (DA)」

NameTitle
PhotoOverview

 

SAP コミュニティバッジの申請方法

SAP Inside Track Tokyoを視聴された方はこちらから申請いただくとご自身のコミュニティIDにバッジが付与されます。せっかくの機会ですので是非ご申請ください。
※SAPコミュニティバッジの申請にはSAPコミュニティにアカウント登録が必要です。
　お持ちでない場合は無料で作成できますのでご登録ください。

Step 1

ご自身が参加予定の回のリンクをクリックしてください
※過去の回には申し込みできませんのでご注意ください
Day 1 
Day 2
Day 3

Step 2

右側にある「あなたは参加しますか？」の質問に対し、「はい」のボタンをクリックしてください

Step 3

「はい」のボタンの下の「参加する」のところにご自身のアイコンが表示されたことをご確認ください

entry.title: Spotkanie Katowickiej Grupy SAP Community - Listopad 2024
entry.content:
Za nami już ostatni SAP Stammtisch w 2024 roku!

Tym razem spotkaliśmy się w pubie Biała Małpa, lecz docelowo nie będzie to nasze miejsce spotkań, ponieważ w planach jest, by następne nasze spotkanie odbyło się w CyberTap w Katowicach.
Wydarzenie jest już dodane do kalendarza katowickiej Grupy, więc serdecznie zapraszamy do zgłaszania chęci uczestnictwa tutaj: katowice-sap-stammtisch-08-01-2025 

Cieszymy się, że na ostatnim spotkaniu Grupy dołączyły do nas osoby spoza regionu. Serdecznie pozdrawiamy Kraków i Poznań oraz zapraszamy ponownie 🙂
Każde spotkanie Grupy SAP Community Katowice, to zawsze ciekawe doświadczenie dla każdego uczestnika. Dlatego też cieszymy się, gdy poza znajomym kręgiem osób pracującym w technologii SAP, pojawia się ktoś nam nieznany, bo takie spotkania zawsze owocują nawiązaniem nowych znajomości oraz uświadamiają, że Świat SAPa, wcale nie jest taki wielki jak może się wydawać i gdzieś w naszej wędrówce zawodowej jest duża szansa przecięcia się z ciekawymi osobami, których spotkało na różnych eventach i z którymi ma się coś wspólnego.
Przyznacie, że ostatnie zdanie wyszło bardzo złożone współrzędnie, więc teraz podsumuję bardzo krótko.


Tak jak zawsze, ostatni SAP Stammtisch w Katowicach można śmiało zaliczyć do udanych 🙂

   

Następny SAP Stammtisch już 8 stycznia. Jak zwykle zapraszamy Was do wcześniejszych zapisów, co pomaga w rezerwacji odpowiedniej wielkości stolika i zachęca innych do przyjścia 🙂

entry.title: Community feedback and contributions in 2025
entry.content:
As we are all slowly starting to wind down 2024 and at the same time prepare for 2025, I wanted to take the opportunity to share some information about community feedback and how you can contribute to the community next year. 

First of all, I would like to start off by assuring everyone that we have heard your feedback this year, both here and on other non-SAP platforms. Everything has been captured. 

Some of the feedback could be attributed to getting used to working on a new platform and getting accustomed to new tools, so we have diligently updated our FAQ/Help Page to help with the most common "how do I" questions we received after the migration earlier this year. The FAQ will of course continuously be updated also in 2025. 

Other feedback was captured as enhancement requests and bugs, and some of these were added to our 2024 releases. More information about these can be found in our What's New section:

  1. June release
  2. August release
  3. October and November releases

We have heard your request for a clearly defined process for how you can provide feedback, and what happens to that feedback once submitted. We are therefore very happy to announce a more transparent feedback process for 2025.

We will start with launching a brand new ideation board in the community. The ideation board will: 

  • give us a better understanding of what matters most to you as you will be able to upvote submitted ideas. 
  • be used if we need clarifications or more information, as we have a shared public space for communication. 
  • give the Community team an opportunity to provide continuous updates on submitted ideas and feedback, as each entry will change status as we process them. (the different statuses are: New, Needs Info, Investigating, Accepted, Declined, Delivered)

To avoid double entries and repeating received feedback, the first version of the ideation board we release will be pre-populated with feedback we have received in 2024, so that you begin voting on what we already have captured. This way we can get experience working with the tool first, and make sure it works as expected both for us and for you. 

If there is feedback we have missed, or you would like to submit new ideas to the board, please send us an email at community@sap.com or use the Questions about SAP Websites Q&A. We will add the ideas to the board so that other community members can see the feedback and vote. 
(a second phase for the ideation board will be to open it for submitting new ideas as well)

The ideation board is still work in progress, and we do not have a confirmed release date yet.
To start the journey of being more transparent about what we are working on, we felt it was a good place to start here, even if it is not 100% ready yet. We will keep you up to date on progress, and of course also share the go-live date as soon as we have it. 
Please be patient with us while we work as fast as we can to add everyone's feedback to the board, with as many details as possible. 

Thank you everyone for your contributions and for keeping the community spirit alive in 2024. 
We are all excited for 2025, making sure the SAP Community continues to thrive together with you.

The entire SAP Community team wishes everyone happy holidays and we look forward to reconnecting in the new year!

entry.title: 🌟 Foi incrível! Obrigado por fazer parte do SAP Inside Track Curitiba 2024! 🌟
entry.content:
Há um mês, nos reunimos para um dia repleto de aprendizado, trocas de experiências e conexões valiosas. O SAP Inside Track Curitiba 2024 superou todas as expectativas, e tudo isso graças a vocês: palestrantes, participantes, patrocinadores e organizadores!

✨Destaques do evento:

Palestras inspiradoras com especialistas que trouxeram as últimas tendências do universo SAP.

Discussões enriquecedoras sobre inovações e desafios do mercado.

Networking que abriu portas para novas parcerias e projetos.

Cases reais de empresas que estão liderando a transformação digital com SAP.

💬E você, o que achou?
Queremos ouvir o seu feedback para que os próximos eventos sejam ainda melhores! 

📆Até o próximo encontro!
Já estamos ansiosos para o próximo SAP Inside Track Curitiba. Fique de olho nas nossas redes para novidades e outros eventos da comunidade.

Mais uma vez, obrigado por fazer parte desta jornada incrível. A comunidade SAP só cresce com a sua participação!

#SITCuritiba #SAPInsideTrack #Gratidão #ComunidadeSAP #Networking

entry.title: Navigating Compliance and Risk in SAP DevSecOps: What Business Leaders Need to Know
entry.content:
As businesses embark on digital transformation journeys, SAP solutions like SAP S/4HANA and RISE with SAP play an important role in streamlining operations and boosting agility. However, in this technological epoch, assuredly the first question rising to the fore is - how do you ensure security and compliance remain at the forefront of the transformation? The integration of DevSecOps in SAP environments offers a path forward. However, its implementation comes with its own set of challenges, particularly in compliance and risk management.

For business leaders, the knowledge to tackle these complexities will allow them not merely to safeguard organizational data but will add to their competitive advantage. The scope of this article will be to discuss the various challenges faced during SAP DevSecOps implementation and how organizations can mitigate security risks, ensure compliance, and maintain operational efficiency while driving innovation.

The Importance of DevSecOps in SAP Environments

DevSecOps is an amalgamation of development, security, and operations. It represents a shift towards embedding security as an integral part of the entire software development lifecycle which is why DevSecOps can no longer be an afterthought at the end of the development process. With the brisk pace of changes in SAP S/4HANA and RISE with SAP, security must be baked in from the beginning.

It is a business prerequisite for leaders to adopt DevSecOps in SAP environments. SAP S/4HANA often serves as the operational backbone of an enterprise, making it a hot target for cyberattacks and compliance risks. Accordingly, businesses must confront compliance and security head-on, ensuring that all components within the SAP ecosystem from cloud platforms to custom applications, meet stringent regulatory standards and move in a high-paced way as per the pains of businesses today.

The Key Challenges in SAP DevSecOps Implementation

While the benefits of a secure, compliant, and agile SAP environment are clear, implementing DevSecOps can be fraught with challenges. Below are some key obstacles businesses face when adopting DevSecOps for SAP.

1. Complexity in SAP Environments

SAP environments, particularly SAP S/4HANA and RISE with SAP, are mostly branched and tailored in different layers. As organizations build up their SAP landscape, they often tend to deploy a mixed bag of on-premises and cloud-based solutions. This hybrid setup can create complexity in securing every facet of the environment, which in turn creates a real challenge in implementing consistent security controls over all platforms.

With the delivery of more cloud-based solutions, ensuring that all cloud services represent SAP’s security and compliance requirements presents a constant challenge. The integration of third-party tools and cloud services into the SAP ecosystem may lead to gaps in security that could otherwise have been avoided, which will only increase the risk exposure.

2. Managing Compliance in a Dynamic Environment

Complying with regulatory authority is one of the most serious tribulations for companies operating in an SAP environment. For organizations using SAP S/4HANA, which houses sensitive financial and operational data, any possible non-compliance might burgeon to hefty fines, legal ramifications, and lifetimes' worth of irreversible reputational sag.

The dynamic nature of SAP implementations makes maintaining constant compliance difficult. As organizations innovate and customize their SAP solutions to meet unique business needs, new features and configurations must be regularly audited. However, ensuring these updates and changes don’t violate compliance requirements is a constant struggle. Additionally, the growing list of global regulations - such as GDPR in Europe or CCPA in California adds complexity to the process.

3. Tool Sprawl and Alert Fatigue

Many businesses encounter a phenomenon known as "tool sprawl," where they deploy a wide array of security tools to manage different aspects of their SAP ecosystem. While this can enhance security capabilities, it often results in an overwhelming number of alerts and reports. Security teams may experience alert fatigue, where the sheer volume of notifications leads to missed critical vulnerabilities or unnecessary focus on non-risk issues.

In an SAP environment, this challenge is particularly pronounced because of the many tools that need to be integrated to secure cloud services, databases, and custom applications. For business leaders, it’s important to establish clear prioritization methods for security alerts and ensure the tools selected integrate seamlessly into the SAP ecosystem.

4. Balancing Speed with Security

According to Gartner, “71% of CISOs say their DevOps stakeholders still view security as an impediment to speed-to-market.”

The demand for faster deployment in SAP environments, especially in projects like RISE with SAP, can lead to conflicts between security and speed. DevSecOps aims to streamline development while embedding security at every stage, but security checks often slow down the deployment process.

For SAP environments, where frequent updates and customizations are needed to keep up with business needs, maintaining a balance between speed and security can be difficult. Business leaders must ensure their development and operations teams don’t see security as a bottleneck but as an enabler of reliable, secure innovation.

Addressing the Challenges in SAP DevSecOps Implementation

Given the complexity of SAP environments, overcoming these challenges requires a structured and strategic approach to DevSecOps. Below are several ways businesses can address the key issues while implementing DevSecOps within SAP.

1. Establishing a Unified Security Framework

Developing a strong DevSecOps strategy involves the integration of security functions throughout the SAP ecosystem into a cohesive security framework. In this case, the framework would be needed to cover the cloud environments, applications, databases, and custom code of the SAP S/4HANA application. With the implementation of a holistic security strategy of this nature comes the opportunity to minimize the risk of unnoticed vulnerabilities and compliance breaches. At the same time, it promotes compliance from the beginning to the end of the software lifecycle.

Among the most important factors in this strategy is automating security testing within the CI/CD pipeline. Several tools, such as SAP Cloud ALM, promote visibility into possible security gaps so that teams can begin remediating such vulnerabilities early on, rather than waiting until deployment.

2. Emphasizing a Clean Core Strategy

For businesses implementing SAP S/4HANA, adhering to a clean core strategy can greatly reduce security risks. A clean core minimizes the customizations made to the core SAP system and pushes custom code and extensions to platforms like SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). This approach not only reduces complexity but also ensures that updates and patches to the core system are seamless, ensuring that security and compliance aren’t compromised during upgrades.

By maintaining a clean core, businesses can more easily implement security measures that apply uniformly across their SAP landscape and reduce the time and resources spent managing complex customizations.

3. Cross-Functional Collaboration and Training

One of the most significant challenges in SAP DevSecOps implementation is overcoming the cultural barriers between development, security, and operations teams. Often, these teams work in silos, each focused on their specific priorities. To address this, businesses must foster a collaborative approach where security is integrated into the entire development and operations process.

Developers and operations teams must understand the security implications of their actions. Security professionals, likewise, must be well-versed in the intricacies of SAP systems and cloud environments. Bridging the knowledge gap between these groups will lead to more efficient risk management and compliance.

4. Continuous Monitoring and Real-Time Feedback

Continuous monitoring is integral to keeping the SAP environment free from threats and compliant with the desired security. Tools like SAP Cloud ALM offer businesses real-time insights into the system’s security posture that enable teams to identify issues and solve them beforehand. It is through a continuous feedback loop that businesses can proactively address vulnerabilities in their SAP environment and ensure that it remains secure, compliant, and agile.

5. Risk-Based Prioritization of Security Efforts

Not all vulnerabilities are equal, and businesses must adopt a risk-based approach to prioritize their security efforts. It is an approach that involves evaluating the severity of vulnerabilities in terms of their potential impact on the business. By focusing on high-risk areas first, organizations can reduce their exposure to major security threats while maintaining operational efficiency.

Looking Ahead: The Future of SAP DevSecOps

As SAP environments continue to evolve, so will the need for robust DevSecOps practices. In the future, businesses can expect more prominence of AI and machine learning in automating security testing, identifying vulnerabilities, and ensuring compliance. With increasingly complex and stringent regulatory compliance, the ability of organizations to adapt to new compliance requirements will easily emerge as a competitive advantage through an integrated security SAP implementation.

Moreover, the ongoing push for cloud adoption will require a more unified and agile approach to security, one that doesn’t go against the dynamism of innovation but pushes it forward, ensuring SAP systems remain resilient and compliant. The next wave of digital transformation will be led by businesses that master these DevSecOps challenges, turning their SAP environments into engines of secure and sustainable growth.

entry.title: Revolutionizing Expense Management: SAP Concur App for Microsoft Teams
entry.content:
We are thrilled to announce the launch of the SAP Concur App for Microsoft Teams, now available in the Microsoft App Store. This powerful integration brings expense management directly into the flow of work, providing employees and approvers with seamless collaboration and enhanced productivity. Here’s a closer look at how this integration is transforming expense management workflows.

Key Features of the SAP Concur App for Microsoft Teams:

  1. View, Share, and Approve Expense Reports: Employees can easily view their most recent active expense reports within Microsoft Teams. The list includes key information such as the status of each report. With just one click, employees can access detailed views of their expense reports in SAP Concur, making the process efficient and user-friendly.

    Sharing expense reports with colleagues or approvers has never been easier. Employees can generate a shareable card for any expense report and forward it to a colleague directly within Teams. The recipient, provided they have the necessary permissions and the SAP Concur App enabled, can view the report and take appropriate action.

    Approvers can quickly access their most recent expense reports awaiting approval through the approval queue in Teams. They can review the details, add comments, and approve or send back reports directly within Teams. All comments and actions are tracked in the audit log in SAP Concur, ensuring transparency and compliance.

  2. Streamlined Employee Actions: Employees can view their expense reports and share them without needing to leave Microsoft Teams, allowing them to stay focused and productive within their workflow.

  3. Simplified Approvals: Approvers save time by reviewing and approving expense reports directly in Teams, with no need to switch applications. This integrated process ensures a smooth and efficient workflow.

How It Works:

  • Sign-In and Connect: Once the SAP Concur App is enabled in Teams, users can sign in and connect their Microsoft Teams account with SAP Concur. This ensures that employees can securely retrieve information from SAP Concur.

  • Intuitive Prompts: Users can interact with the app by typing prompts or selecting actions from a list of available prompts. Whether it’s viewing reports, sharing them, or approving them, the app provides clear and simple workflows.

Prerequisites for Using the SAP Concur for Microsoft Teams App:

To use the SAP Concur for Microsoft Teams app seamlessly, your organization must:

  • Have a contract with SAP Concur and have the Concur Travel and/or Concur Expense solution enabled.

  • Be eligible to and opted into the new Concur Expense and its new booking experience.

  • Get an Administrator to activate the app within your Microsoft environment.

For more information on how to get started, check the SAP Concur help portal for Microsoft Teams.

The Value of Integration:

The SAP Concur App for Microsoft Teams eliminates the need to switch between applications, saving time and improving focus. By bringing expense management into the Teams environment, employees and approvers can:

  • Improve efficiency by managing expenses within their existing collaboration tools.

  • Enhance transparency with easy sharing and tracking of expense reports.

  • Boost productivity by reducing friction in the expense approval process.

This integration is a game-changer for organizations looking to modernize their expense management practices and empower employees with tools that adapt to their workflows.

Ready to Get Started?

The SAP Concur App for Microsoft Teams is now available in the Microsoft App Store. Enable it today and experience the future of seamless expense management. Together, let’s make expense management smarter, faster, and more collaborative.

entry.title: Understanding NACE Transaction Code in SAP
entry.content:
In SAP, effective document management is essential for seamless business operations. Transaction code NACE serves as a critical tool for configuring and managing output determination, enabling organizations to generate and distribute documents such as invoices, purchase orders, and delivery notes. This blog provides a comprehensive overview of NACE and its applications. 

In SAP, the output management process plays a crucial role in generating and distributing business documents like invoices, purchase orders, and delivery notes. One of the primary tools used for configuring these outputs is the NACE transaction code. This blog will guide you through the process of creating and customizing an output type in SAP using NACE. 

NACE T-code  

NACE is a transaction code in SAP ABAP used for output determination. It helps in configuring and managing the output types for various documents like purchase orders, sales orders, delivery notes, and billing documents. Here's a brief overview of its components: 

  • Application: Defines the type of document (e.g., EF for purchase orders, V1 for sales documents). 
  • Output Types: Specifies the format and medium of the output (e.g., print, email, fax). 
  • Processing Routines: Links the output type to the driver program and the form (e.g., SAP script or Smart form) used for generating the output. 

Using NACE, you can dynamically determine the output based on various conditions, making it a flexible tool for managing document outputs in SAP. 

Key Features of NACE 

Centralized Configuration: Simplifies the setup and management of output determination for different business processes. 

Support for Multiple Application Areas: Covers diverse areas such as sales (V1), purchasing (EF), and inventory management (ML). 

Customizable Output Types: Allows the creation and configuration of custom output types tailored to business needs. 

Integration with Forms: Supports integration with SAP script, Smart Forms, and Adobe Forms for document formatting. 

SAP Output Processing – SAP Output Message with SAP NACE 

This article explains how to create a new SAP Output Message using the NACE T-code. It provides a step-by-step tutorial covering all the necessary customizations to set up a fully functional output message type from scratch. 

Create a new sap output message Type 

Step 1: Call NACE T-code and Go to Output Types 

Launch the SAP Transaction Code NACE for Conditions for Output Control. 
First let’s select the Output Types as following and The Application (for Sales, Application is V1)

Step 2: Change display mode to edit mode then Create New entry

Step 3: Fill ID of SAP Output Message Type, description and access type

Create the output type with a custom name starting with "Z." 

Step 4: Set NACE Output Type Dispatch Time for? sap output message 

You can process a message immediately by updating the application document or you can process it at a later time. For later processing you must use the report program RSNAST00? or a corresponding application function.

  • Send with periodically scheduled job: The message is processed through report program RSNAST00. This report program can be scheduled periodically or started manually 
  • Send with job, with additional time specification: As in time 1, but the message contains an earliest processing date and a requested processing time. The message is ignored by report program RSNAST00 before the requested date. 
  • Send with application own transaction: The message is selected and processed by application specific programs. Processing can take place online or in the background 
  • Send immediately (when saving the application): The message is processed when the application document is saved. No further processing of the message is necessary.

Step 5: Set Message Transmission Medium Specifies how the output will be produced. 

You can specify, for example, that the output is printed or that it is produced and sent by other means, such as fax, electronic mail, or electronic data interchange (EDI/ IDoc). ￼

Step 6: Set Partner Function a sample list of used function partner in Output Type

SP: Sold-To Party 

BP: Bill-to Party 

SH: Ship-To Party 

PY: Payer 

LS: Logical System? 

Etc....... 

Step 7: Set Processing routines 

For each Transmission Medium, a processing routine must be configured. For this sample, the Output Type triggers an IDoc with the program RSNASTED within the routine EDI_PROCESSING.

You can leave all the rest of tabs blank.
When Saving, SAP will ask you for customization request. (Good point: Nace Configuration in transportable)

Step 8: Set Partner Functions Determination

Here by clicking the 'New Entries' fill the value as shown below

Step 9: Create Condition Records 

Go Back to NACE transaction and select Condition Records 
Remember we have configured the Access sequence defined in a previous step, create condition for output type. ￼Step 10: Create Output Procedure

Go Back to NACE transaction and select Procedures 

Step 11: Assign Output Procedure to transaction type

Go to SPRO Customizing and find where the customizing point to maintain Output Determination

Test Output Message 

here the last step. test your output message and check if the SAP Output processing in well done.

(For Order, for example? try to modify an SAP order, then save. Go to message > Header > and check the output processing if it is green)

Troubleshooting 

if Message Output doesn't appear in header > message on Transaction, verify this point:

  • Is record conditions meet the criteria of the Transaction?
  • Is record conditions maintained?

if you can add manually the Output type you have created, make sure this Output is used on output procedure (NACE) Active for the transaction type (SPRO)

Conclusion  

Transaction code NACE is a powerful tool for configuring output determination in SAP. By following the steps outlined in this blog, you can create and manage output types effectively, ensuring smooth and automated document processing for your business processes. Whether you are dealing with invoices, purchase orders, or delivery notes, NACE provides the flexibility and control needed to meet your organization's requirements.

entry.title: Understanding SAP IDocs
entry.content:
SAP IDocs (Intermediate Documents) are a standardized format for data exchange between SAP systems and external systems. They are used to transfer data in a structured and reliable way.

Commonly Used SAP IDoc Types
While there are numerous IDoc types, here are some of the most frequently used:
* ORDERS: Used for purchase orders.
* INVOIC: Used for invoices.
* DESADV: Used for delivery schedules.
* MATMAS: Used for material master data.

Key Points to Remember:
* IDoc Structure: IDocs have a specific structure, including segments and fields, which defines the data to be transferred.
* IDoc Types: Different IDoc types have different structures tailored to specific business processes.
* IDoc Processing: IDocs are processed through various steps, including inbound processing, application processing, and outbound processing.
* ALE (Application Link Enabling): ALE is the framework used to exchange IDocs between SAP systems.
* EDI (Electronic Data Interchange): IDocs can be used for EDI, allowing data exchange with external systems in standard formats.

SAP IDoc: 50 Transaction Codes You Need To Know
Find below a list of the SAP IDoc & EDI related transaction codes which I personally find useful.

SAP IDoc: 50 Transaction Codes You Need To Know
Find below a list of the SAP IDoc & EDI related transaction codes which I personally find useful.


BD51 Maintain function modules (inbound)
BD55 Maintain IDoc Conversion
BD62 Define Segment Conversion Rule
BD79 Maintain IDoc Conversion Rules
BD87 Status Monitor for ALE Messages
BDM2 Monitoring: IDocs in Receiving Sys.
BDM7 ALE Audit: statistical analyses
IDOC IDoc: Repair and check programs
SM58 Asynchronous RFC Error Log
SM59 RFC Destinations (Display/Maintain)
WE02 Display IDoc
WE05 IDoc Lists
WE06 Active IDoc monitoring
WE07 IDoc statistics
WE09 Search for IDocs by Content
WE10 Search for IDocs by Content
WE11 Delete IDocs
WE12 Test Modified Inbound File
WE14 Test Outbound Processing
WE15 Test Outbound Processing from MC
WE16 Test Inbound File
WE17 Test Status File
WE18 Create Status File
WE19 Test tool
WE20 Partner Profiles
WE21 Port definition
WE24 DefaultValuesForOutboundParameters
WE27 DefaultValues for Inbound Parameters
WE30 IDoc Type Development
WE31 Development IDoc Segment
WE32 Development IDoc View
WE34 Object for Display of XML IDocs
WE41 Process codes, outbound
WE42 Process codes, inbound
WE43 Funct.module: Status record display
WE44 Partner Types and Checks
WE45 Forward (inbound) (V3, EDILOGADR)
WE47 Status Maintenance
WE55 Function Module for Path Names
WE57 Assignment Messages for Appl. Objs
WE60 Documentation for IDoc types
WE64 Documentation message types
WE70 Conversion: Basic types
WE71 Conversion: Extensions
WE72 Conversion: IDoc types
WE73 Conversion: Logical messages
WE81 Logical message types
WE82 Assign Messages for IDoc Type
WELI Define Status Groups
WLF_IDOC IDoc Processing

entry.title: IDoc Failure and Resolution in SAP
entry.content:
IDocs (Intermediate Documents) in SAP are used for Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) to exchange data between SAP and external systems. However, IDoc processing can encounter errors resulting in failures. Here's a breakdown of IDoc failures and their resolution process, along with an example:

**IDoc Failure:**

An IDoc failure occurs when there's an issue during data exchange, preventing successful transfer of information. This can happen due to various reasons:

* **Data Mapping Errors:** Incorrect mapping between SAP data fields and the external system's format.
* **Missing or Invalid Data:** Required data fields missing in the IDoc or containing invalid values.
* **Communication Issues:** Network connectivity problems between SAP and the external system.
* **Application Errors:** Errors in the receiving application logic causing processing issues.

**Resolving IDoc Failures:**

Here's a general process for resolving IDoc failures:

1. **Identify the Error:** Use transaction WE05 (Display IDoc) to check the IDoc status and error message. The message provides details about the cause of the failure.
2. **Analyze the Error:** Based on the error message, pinpoint the root cause (data mapping, missing data, etc.).
3. **Resolve the Issue:**
* Fix data mapping errors in the configuration.
* Correct missing or invalid data in the originating document.
* Troubleshoot network connectivity issues.
* Work with the application team to address application-specific errors.
4. **Reprocess the IDoc:** Once the issue is resolved, use transaction WE19 (Reprocess IDoc) to attempt processing the IDoc again.

**Example:**

An IDoc for a sales order fails with an error message indicating a missing customer number. The resolution steps might involve:

* Checking the originating sales order in transaction VA01.
* Verifying if the customer number is missing or incorrect.
* Updating the customer number in the sales order.
* Reprocessing the IDoc using WE19.

**Additional Tips:**

* Use transaction SM37 (Job Overview) to monitor background jobs related to IDoc processing.
* Leverage table EDIDS to track IDoc statuses and detailed error logs.
* For complex errors, consider involving SAP Basis or EDI specialists for further assistance.

By following these steps and understanding the potential causes of IDoc failures, you can effectively troubleshoot and resolve issues to ensure smooth data exchange between SAP and external systems.

entry.title: Defining Local Fields in SAP QUERY
entry.content:
Local Fields in SAP Query: Enhancing Reporting Flexibility

Local fields in SAP Query are calculated fields that you define within the query itself. They don't exist in the underlying database tables or InfoSet. This allows you to perform calculations, transformations, or logical operations on existing fields without needing to create new fields in the database. This is very useful for reporting purposes.

Key Benefits of Local Fields:

  • Flexibility: Create custom calculations and derive new information without modifying the underlying data structure.
  • Performance: Calculations are performed during query execution, avoiding the need to store redundant data.
  • Simplicity: Define complex logic directly within the query, making it easier to manage and maintain.

Example: Calculating Delivery Variance (Your Scenario)

Let's use your example of the BACKLOGVENTES InfoSet to illustrate how to define a local field for delivery variance:

Scenario: You want to see the difference between the referenced quantity (DELIVERY) and the confirmed quantity (SALES ORDER). This difference represents the delivery variance.

Steps to Define a Local Field:

  1. Open SAP Query (SQ01/SQ02): Navigate to SAP Query using transaction codes SQ01 (Queries) or SQ02 (InfoSets).

  2. Edit/Create a Query: Open the relevant query based on the BACKLOGVENTES InfoSet.

  3. Go to the "Fields" tab: This is where you select the fields to be included in your report.

  4. Define a Local Field:

    • Click on the "Local Fields" button (usually represented by an icon of a calculator or a field with a plus sign).

    • Enter a name for your local field (e.g., DELIVERY_VARIANCE).

    • Enter a description (e.g., "Difference between Referenced and Confirmed Quantity").

    • Crucially, enter the formula: This is where you define the calculation. In your case, the formula would be:

      DELIVERY - SALES_ORDER

      (Assuming DELIVERY represents the "Referenced quantity in base unit of measure" and SALES_ORDER represents the "Confirmed Quantity". Adjust the field names to match your InfoSet.)

    • Specify the data type and length of the local field, if necessary. Usually, the query tool infers this based on the formula.

  5. Add the Local Field to the Output: Select the newly created local field (DELIVERY_VARIANCE) to be included in your report output.

  6. Execute the Query: Run the query to see the results, including the calculated delivery variance.

Example in More Detail (with potential field names):

Let's say the actual field names in your InfoSet are:

  • VBAP-KWMENG (Confirmed Quantity in Sales Order - often aliased as SALES_ORDER in the Infoset)
  • LIPS-LFIMG (Delivered Quantity / Referenced Quantity in delivery - often aliased as DELIVERY)

Then, your local field definition would be:

  • Name: DELIVERY_VARIANCE
  • Description: "Difference between Delivered and Confirmed Quantity"
  • Formula: LIPS-LFIMG - VBAP-KWMENG or DELIVERY - SALES_ORDER (depending on how the fields are aliased in your InfoSet)

Important Considerations:

  • Data Types: Ensure the data types of the fields used in the formula are compatible. You might need to use type conversions (e.g., NUMC to DEC) if necessary.
  • Aliases: If your InfoSet uses aliases for field names, use the aliases in your formula.
  • Error Handling: Consider potential errors, such as dividing by zero. You can use conditional statements (e.g., IF...THEN...ELSE) to handle these cases.

By using local fields effectively, you can create powerful and flexible reports in SAP Query without needing to modify the underlying data structure, making reporting much easier and more efficient.

entry.title: Netweaver Versions and EHP Versions Explained
entry.content:
Dear Guru's,

Happy New Year Everyone. Wishing Everyone a Healthy and Happy Life.

I have always faced issues or in understanding the Netweaver and EHP Versions while doing Upgrades or Netweaver Stack Upgrades.
Here I wanted to give a short information with respect to Netweaver Versions for understanding purpose which might help someone in getting a clear picture. Hope it Helps.
ECC --> Enterprise Core Component starts with ECC 6.0, ECC 6.0 EHP1 till ECC 6.0 EHP8.
NW --> Netweaver starts with Netweaver 7.0 till Netweaver 7.52.

Below are few information On ECC and Netweaver Versions.
ECC Versions with EHP8 Details:

S.NoYear ReleasedECC Versions
12016ECC 6 EHP8
22013ECC 6 EHP7
32011ECC 6 EHP6
42010ECC 6 EHP5
52008ECC 6 EHP4
62007ECC 6 EHP3
72007ECC 6 EHP2
82006ECC 6 EHP1
92005ECC 6.0

 
Netweaver Versions:

S.NoYear ReleasedNetweaver VersionsComments
120157.5 ==> 7.50NW 7.5
220137.4 ==> 7.50NW 7.4
320117.3 EHP1 ==> 7.31NW 7.3 EHP1
420107.3 ==> 7.30NW 7.3
520097.2 ==> 7.20NW 7.2
620097.1 EHP1 ==> 711NW 7.1 EHP1
720077.1 ==> 710NW 7.1
820117.0 EHP3 ==> 703NW 7.0 EHP3
920107.0 EHP2 ==> 702NW 7.0 EHP2
1020087.0 EHP1 ==> 701NW 7.0 EHP1
1120057.0 ==> 700NW 7.0


Netweaver 701 is included under NW 7.0 EHP1 same for NW 702 where same represents NW 7.0 EHP2. Here Enhancement Package is released for NW 7.0 alone 3 times say NW 7.0 EHP1, NW 7.0 EHP2 and NW 7.0 EHP3 where they read as NW 701, NW 702 and NW 703.

Same Applies for NW 7.31 where EHP1 released for NW 7.31.

It can be taken as like an Support Package or Patch Level versions kind of thing for understanding purpose.

Kindly do pour in your comments if any information is wrong or anything to be corrected.

Thanks and Regards,
Mithran

entry.title: Converting Rows to Columns in a CDS Report in S/4HANA Cloud
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, it is common to encounter scenarios where different types of data are stored in the same column across multiple rows. To meet reporting requirements, this data often needs to be displayed in separate columns on the same row. This article demonstrates how to achieve this transformation using Custom CDS Views.

Example Scenario: Tax Data Conversion

Consider a table where various tax components (e.g., Total Amount, Base Amount, IGST, CGST, SGST) are stored in a single column, AmountinCoCodeCurrency, but on different rows. The goal is to display these values in distinct columns in the same row for a CDS report.

Input Table

Company Code

Journal Entry

Fiscal Year

Posting View Item

AmountinCoCodeCurrency

Line Item ID

Transaction Key

Financial Account Type

1110

94000001

2024

1

1250

 

 

K

1110

94000001

2024

2

1050

S

 

 

1110

94000001

2024

3

100

T

JIS

 

1110

94000001

2024

4

100

T

JIC

 

1110

94000001

2024

5

0

T

JII

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Output Table


Company Code

Journal Entry

Fiscal Year

Total Amount

Base Amount

IGST

CGST

SGST

1110

94000001

2024

1250

1050

100

100

0

 

Steps to Achieve the Transformation

  1. Create Custom CDS Views for Each Tax Component

For each tax component, create a Custom CDS View to extract its specific data. Use a unique filter condition to isolate the relevant rows.

  • Base Amount:
    Filter: Line Item ID = 'S' and Financial Account Type != 'K'.
  • Total Amount:
    Filter: Financial Account Type = 'K'.
  • IGST:
    Filter: Transaction Key = 'JIS'.
  • CGST:
    Filter: Transaction Key = 'JIC'.
  • SGST:
    Filter: Transaction Key = 'JII'.
  1. Combine CDS Views

After creating individual CDS Views for each tax component, combine them into a single Custom CDS View using an Inner Join. Use common fields such as Company Code, Journal Entry, and Fiscal Year as the join conditions to align the data.

  1. Define the Output Structure

The resulting CDS View should include columns for:

  • Company Code
  • Journal Entry
  • Fiscal Year
  • Total Amount
  • Base Amount
  • IGST
  • CGST
  • SGST

Key Considerations

  • Filter Design: Ensure that each tax component has a unique identifying filter to avoid overlapping or missing data.
  • Join Conditions: Use precise join conditions to maintain data integrity and avoid duplication.
  • Performance: Test the combined CDS View for performance, especially with large datasets. Optimize as needed.

By following this approach, you can efficiently transform row-based data into columnar format in a CDS Report, as illustrated in the example. This method provides flexibility for customizing reports and meeting business requirements in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.
